description: Explains how marimo connects interactive UI elements, cell reactivity, markdown and rich outputs, and imperative output updates for app-like notebooks.
document:
marimo treats notebook UI, visual output, and reactivity as one connected model. The guides explain that every cell may have a visual output, shown above the cell while editing and arranged as the notebook's interface when run as an app. By default, that visual output is the last expression in the cell, so a markdown object, a layout, a media object, a table, or a UI element can become part of the app without a separate display call. Console text such as print output is deliberately separate: it appears below the cell while editing and is not included in app view unless the user captures or redirects it into the visual output area.

Interactive elements live under `marimo.ui` and are implemented around the `UIElement` base class in the plugin core. Each element has a frontend value that must be JSON-serializable and a Python-side value exposed through its value property. Subclasses convert frontend values into the Python value they want notebook code to consume. During construction, an element receives a stable object id from the runtime when possible, registers itself with the UI element registry, and emits a custom web component wrapped in marimo's HTML output. The stable id synchronizes browser instances with the kernel, while a separate random token changes whenever the creating cell reruns so the frontend knows to reset to the new initial value.

The interaction rule is intentionally simple: when a UI element assigned to a global variable changes in the browser, marimo sends the new value back to Python and automatically runs cells that reference that global variable without defining it. The cell that defines the element is not rerun because it owns the element. This is why the docs stress that interactions only affect the notebook when the element is bound to a global variable. The value is read from another cell through the element's value property, and the implementation prevents reading that value from the same cell that created the element so authors do not accidentally depend on a not-yet-interacted object.

Output composition is built on `Html`, the wrapper used for renderable HTML fragments. Markdown from `mo.md` returns an HTML object after processing extensions for code highlighting, math, tables, admonition-style blocks, details, emojis, links, and related rich text features. marimo makes HTML objects and UI elements interpolate cleanly into markdown strings, which is why a slider or text input can be embedded directly inside a prose prompt. Other Python objects can be adapted through `mo.as_html`, which asks the formatter system for an appropriate MIME representation and then turns that representation into embeddable HTML. In noninteractive contexts, markdown can preserve a markdown representation instead of always returning browser HTML.

The formatter layer keeps ordinary Python objects useful as cell outputs. Registered formatters and object display protocols take precedence, and the fallback is a plain textual representation. Opinionated formatters are enabled in interactive use so common data objects can render richly, but `mo.plain` lets an author opt out of those enhanced displays. Layout and stateless output plugins build on the same HTML pathway: stacks convert children through the HTML conversion helper and keep live references for mutable outputs, while callouts, media, tabs, sidebars, navigation, inspection, and progress indicators all become renderable output objects.

marimo also supports output updates while a cell is still running. The runtime output helpers replace, append, clear, or replace an item in the current cell's output list, then broadcast the formatted result to the frontend. Appended outputs are stacked vertically through the same layout machinery, and an explicit last expression that is not None replaces any earlier imperative output unless the author appends it. For console streams, capture helpers return buffers for later use, while redirect helpers send writes into the cell output area as plain text. `mo.show_code` is a related output helper for app views: it displays the current cell's code alongside an optional rendered result, using a disabled code editor component.
